content.text: At the heart of building LLM-powered applications lies a fundamental challenge: a single prompt-response cycle is rarely enough. Real applications need to call models multiple times, transform outputs, fetch data, and route logic — all in a coherent, reusable way. Chains are LangChain's answer to this: a structured mechanism for composing sequences of operations into pipelines that can be built once and reused at scale.

in LangChain is a composable unit that links together one or more components — prompts, language models, output parsers, retrievers, tools, or even other chains — into a single callable pipeline. When you invoke a chain, data flows through each component in sequence, with the output of one step becoming the input of the next.
LangChain has two generations of chain architecture:
, etc. These still work but are considered legacy as of LangChain v0.2+.
— The modern, preferred approach introduced in 2023. Chains are built by composing components using the pipe operator
The vast majority of production LangChain code written today uses LCEL, and it is what Anthropic, OpenAI, and the LangChain team recommend for new projects.

Without chains, every LLM application would require manual orchestration: formatting prompts by hand, passing outputs to models, parsing results, feeding them into the next step, and handling errors at each junction. This produces brittle, hard-to-maintain code.
— Define a pipeline once, call it many times with different inputs.
— Small chains can be nested inside larger chains, enabling modular design.
— Prompts, models, parsers, and retrievers each live in their own component; chains wire them together.
— LCEL chains support token streaming out of the box without extra code.
— LangChain's tracing integrations (LangSmith) work automatically at the chain level.
— LCEL chains have native async/await support for concurrent execution.
— LCEL pipelines read almost like a sentence:
to process multiple inputs in parallel automatically.
— Chains automatically know their input/output types for validation.
— Built-in retry mechanisms at the component level.
— Each component in a chain can be swapped for a mock, making unit testing straightforward.
— LCEL's pipe-based syntax feels unfamiliar to developers used to imperative Python.
— When a multi-step chain fails, pinpointing which step caused the issue requires tracing tools or verbose logging.
— For very simple use cases (single prompt → single model), chains add boilerplate that is overkill.
— LangChain's API has changed significantly across versions; legacy chain patterns and LCEL patterns coexist, causing documentation confusion.
composition adds a thin layer of Python object wrapping. For extremely high-throughput applications this may be a factor, though in practice the LLM API call dominates latency by orders of magnitude.
Every LCEL component — whether it is a prompt template, a model, or a parser — implements the
. This means every component exposes the same set of methods:
Because every component shares this interface, they are composable with
— the pipe operator — which is implemented as
on the Runnable class. When you write:
that, when invoked, calls each component in order and passes the output of one as the input to the next.
Input arrives as a Python dict (for multi-variable chains) or a single value.
formats the dict into a string or list of messages.
receives the formatted prompt and returns a response object (e.g.,
transforms the response object into a usable Python type (string, dict, Pydantic model, list, etc.).
The final parsed output is returned to the caller.

User Input (dict or string)
        │
        ▼
┌───────────────────┐
│   PromptTemplate  │  ← Formats input variables into a prompt
│  (Runnable)       │
└────────┬──────────┘
         │  Formatted prompt (string or messages)
         ▼
┌───────────────────┐
│   ChatModel / LLM │  ← Sends prompt to LLM API, receives response
│  (Runnable)       │
└────────┬──────────┘
         │  AIMessage / LLMResult
         ▼
┌───────────────────┐
│   OutputParser    │  ← Parses raw LLM output into structured data
│  (Runnable)       │
└────────┬──────────┘
         │
         ▼
     Final Output
For more complex architectures, chains are nested:
The most fundamental pattern: format a prompt, call a model, parse the result.

from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template(
    "Explain {topic} in simple terms."
)
model = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o")
parser = StrOutputParser()

chain = prompt | model | parser

result = chain.invoke({"topic": "transformer attention mechanism"})
print(result)
Chain the output of one sub-chain as the input to another. In LCEL, this is done by composing a

from langchain_core.runnables import RunnablePassthrough

# Step 1: Generate a joke
joke_chain = (
    ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("Tell me a joke about {topic}")
    | model
    | StrOutputParser()
)

# Step 2: Explain why the joke is funny
explain_chain = (
    ChatPromptTemplate.from_template(
        "Explain why this joke is funny: {joke}"
    )
    | model
    | StrOutputParser()
)

# Compose: pass original output forward as 'joke' key
full_chain = (
    joke_chain
    | (lambda joke: {"joke": joke})
    | explain_chain
)

result = full_chain.invoke({"topic": "programmers"})
The most common production chain type. Retrieves relevant documents from a vector store and includes them in the prompt as context before calling the model.
Run multiple chains concurrently and collect all results into a single dict. This is useful when you need to generate multiple perspectives or summaries at the same time.

from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableParallel

parallel_chain = RunnableParallel(
    summary=summary_chain,
    sentiment=sentiment_chain,
    keywords=keyword_chain
)

results = parallel_chain.invoke({"text": "The product arrived late but worked perfectly."})
# results = {"summary": "...", "sentiment": "...", "keywords": [...]}
Route inputs to different chains depending on a condition. This is the LCEL equivalent of the legacy

from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableBranch

branch = RunnableBranch(
    (lambda x: x["language"] == "french", french_chain),
    (lambda x: x["language"] == "spanish", spanish_chain),
    default_chain   # fallback
)

result = branch.invoke({"language": "french", "question": "Bonjour?"})
Force the model to return output that conforms to a Pydantic schema. This is critical for downstream programmatic use.

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

class MovieReview(BaseModel):
    title: str = Field(description="Movie title")
    rating: int = Field(description="Rating from 1 to 10")
    summary: str = Field(description="Brief review summary")

model = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o")
structured_model = model.with_structured_output(MovieReview)

chain = prompt | structured_model

review = chain.invoke({"movie": "Inception"})
print(review.rating)   # Typed Python object, not raw string
You need more than one step to produce a useful result (e.g., retrieve → summarize → format).
You want to reuse the same pipeline for many different inputs.
You need streaming, batching, or async support without writing it from scratch.
You are building RAG pipelines, document Q&A, translation pipelines, or multi-step content generation workflows.
You want structured, validated output from a model.
is sufficient — adding a chain is unnecessary overhead.
You need deeply dynamic, self-directed behavior where the model decides what actions to take at runtime — that is the domain of
You are not using LangChain at all; in that case, build the pipeline in plain Python or use a simpler framework.

import os
os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"] = "your-api-key"

from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
model = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o", temperature=0.7)

from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are a helpful assistant who explains things simply."),
    ("human", "Explain {concept} in one paragraph.")
])

from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser
parser = StrOutputParser()
Step 5: Compose the Chain with LCEL

# Single call
result = chain.invoke({"concept": "gradient descent"})

# Streaming
for chunk in chain.stream({"concept": "neural networks"}):
    print(chunk, end="", flush=True)

# Batch
results = chain.batch([
    {"concept": "backpropagation"},
    {"concept": "overfitting"},
    {"concept": "dropout"}
])

fallback_model = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-3.5-turbo")
fallback_chain = prompt | fallback_model | parser

robust_chain = chain.with_fallbacks([fallback_chain])

from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser

summarize_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template(
    "Summarize the following text in 3 bullet points:\n\n{text}"
)

summarize_chain = summarize_prompt | ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o") | StrOutputParser()

article = """
Large language models (LLMs) are deep learning models trained on massive 
text corpora. They use transformer architecture and attention mechanisms 
to understand and generate human-like text. Applications include chatbots, 
code generation, translation, summarization, and reasoning tasks.
"""

print(summarize_chain.invoke({"text": article}))

from pydantic import BaseModel

class TranslationResult(BaseModel):
    original: str
    translated: str
    target_language: str

translate_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template(
    "Translate the following text to {language}: {text}"
)

structured_model = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o").with_structured_output(TranslationResult)

translate_chain = translate_prompt | structured_model

result = translate_chain.invoke({
    "text": "Hello, how are you?",
    "language": "French"
})

print(result.translated)        # "Bonjour, comment allez-vous?"
print(result.target_language)   # "French"
Real-World Example: Customer Support RAG Pipeline
A real production deployment at a SaaS company handles thousands of support queries per day. The architecture uses a multi-step chain:

User Query
    │
    ▼
Intent Classification Chain  ←── Classifies query as: billing / technical / general
    │
    ├──► Billing Chain  ──► Billing KB Retriever ──► Prompt ──► Model ──► Answer
    │
    ├──► Technical Chain ──► Tech Docs Retriever ──► Prompt ──► Model ──► Answer
    │
    └──► General Chain  ──► General KB Retriever ──► Prompt ──► Model ──► Answer

from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableBranch, RunnablePassthrough, RunnableParallel
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser

model = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o")

# --- Classifiers and per-domain chains ---
classify_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template(
    "Classify this support query as 'billing', 'technical', or 'general'. "
    "Respond with one word only.\n\nQuery: {query}"
)

classify_chain = classify_prompt | model | StrOutputParser()

def make_rag_chain(retriever, system_msg):
    prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
        ("system", system_msg),
        ("human", "Context:\n{context}\n\nQuestion: {question}")
    ])
    return (
        {
            "context": retriever | (lambda docs: "\n".join(d.page_content for d in docs)),
            "question": RunnablePassthrough()
        }
        | prompt | model | StrOutputParser()
    )

billing_chain = make_rag_chain(billing_retriever, "You are a billing support specialist.")
technical_chain = make_rag_chain(tech_retriever, "You are a technical support engineer.")
general_chain = make_rag_chain(general_retriever, "You are a helpful support agent.")

# --- Router ---
def route(x):
    category = classify_chain.invoke({"query": x["query"]}).strip().lower()
    if category == "billing":
        return billing_chain
    elif category == "technical":
        return technical_chain
    return general_chain

from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableLambda

full_chain = RunnableLambda(lambda x: route(x).invoke(x["query"]))

answer = full_chain.invoke({"query": "Why was I charged twice this month?"})
This pipeline runs with LangSmith tracing enabled, giving the team per-step latency metrics, input/output logs, and error rates for each chain component individually.
syntax causes subtle incompatibilities. Pick one approach per project; prefer LCEL for new code.
When composing two chains sequentially, the output of the first chain must match the expected input schema of the second. A missing
wrapper is a very common runtime error.

# WRONG - second chain expects a dict with key 'joke', but first returns a plain string
chain = joke_chain | explain_chain

# RIGHT
chain = joke_chain | (lambda x: {"joke": x}) | explain_chain
call does not need to be wrapped in a chain. Only chain when there are genuinely multiple steps or reuse requirements.
Relying on string parsing with regex or
to extract data from LLM responses is fragile. Use
for any data that needs to be consumed programmatically.
with no extra work. Not using it in user-facing products results in an awkward wait before any output appears.
objects, not as f-strings inside lambdas. This makes them overridable, testable, and traceable.
are deprecated and will be removed in future versions.
to inject computed values into the input dict without discarding original keys.

from langchain_core.runnables import RunnablePassthrough

chain = RunnablePassthrough.assign(
    summary=summarize_chain
) | final_prompt | model | StrOutputParser()
— wrap chain inputs in a Pydantic model and use
on API-calling components to handle transient rate limits automatically.
from the start, not after debugging becomes painful:
Keep individual chains small and single-purpose.
Compose small chains into larger ones rather than building one monolithic chain.
in a loop — it parallelizes requests with configurable concurrency.

The dominant cost in any LangChain chain is the LLM API call, which takes hundreds of milliseconds to several seconds. Chain overhead itself (Python object construction, Runnable wrapping) is microseconds and almost never a bottleneck.
reduces perceived latency in user-facing apps from "wait 3 seconds then see everything" to "start seeing tokens in 200ms."
under the hood to parallelize multiple LLM calls, dramatically reducing total wall-clock time for bulk workloads.

# Sequential: 10 calls × 2s each = 20s total
for inp in inputs:
    chain.invoke(inp)

# Batch: 10 calls in parallel = ~2–3s total
chain.batch(inputs, config={"max_concurrency": 5})
at the LLM level reduces redundant API calls for identical prompts:

from langchain_core.globals import set_llm_cache
from langchain_community.cache import InMemoryCache

set_llm_cache(InMemoryCache())
# Second call with the same prompt returns instantly from cache
instead of in-memory caching so the cache survives process restarts.
is a key pattern for RAG chains that query multiple sources simultaneously rather than sequentially.
Tracing, debugging, evaluating, and monitoring chains in production
Deploy any LCEL chain as a REST API (FastAPI-based) with one function call
Build stateful, cyclic, multi-agent workflows; uses chains as nodes
Vector stores commonly used as retrievers inside RAG chains
Repository of reusable, community-vetted prompt templates usable in chains
The underlying model providers; LangChain wraps them in a unified interface

from fastapi import FastAPI
from langserve import add_routes

app = FastAPI()
add_routes(app, chain, path="/summarize")

# Now accessible at POST /summarize/invoke, /summarize/stream, /summarize/batch

LangChain LCEL is the most widely adopted and has the richest ecosystem, but LlamaIndex is a strong competitor for document-heavy RAG use cases, and DSPy is compelling when automatic prompt optimization matters more than composability.

— Chains that include user-provided text in prompts are vulnerable to prompt injection attacks where a malicious user embeds instructions that override the system prompt. Mitigations include sanitizing user input, using structured output formats that do not execute arbitrary instructions, and sandboxing any tool-calling steps.

— Keys must never be hardcoded in chain definitions or logged by tracing tools. Use environment variables or secret managers. Verify that LangSmith trace logs are configured to redact sensitive fields.
— A poorly designed sequential chain can pass enormous contexts between steps, causing unexpected token usage and cost. Set
limits on model components and add context compression steps where needed.
— Retrieved documents may be irrelevant or misleading. Always validate retrieval quality and consider adding a re-ranker step before passing documents to the model.
with exponential backoff on model components to handle provider rate limits gracefully without crashing the chain.
— In multi-step chains, a failure at any step propagates immediately. Use
to provide degraded-but-functional backup paths for critical chains.
Think of a LangChain chain as an

. Each station on the line does one specific job — stamp, drill, paint, inspect — and passes the part to the next station. The assembly line as a whole produces a finished product, and any station can be replaced or upgraded without rebuilding the entire line. LCEL's

operator is simply the conveyor belt connecting the stations. The Runnable interface ensures that every station accepts and returns parts in a standard format, so any station can connect to any other.
is a composable pipeline that links prompts, models, parsers, and retrievers into a single callable unit.
syntax) is the modern, preferred way to build chains; legacy class-based chains are deprecated.
, giving all chains built-in support for
, which retrieve documents and include them as context before calling the model, are the most common production chain pattern.
routes inputs to different chains based on conditions.
whenever the model's response needs to be consumed programmatically — do not parse raw strings.
is the essential companion tool for debugging, tracing, and monitoring chains in production.
Chains are the right tool for deterministic, predefined pipelines; when the model needs to decide what steps to take dynamically, that is the role of
